explicit_text: I was born in Jacksonville on May 27, 1869, and spent my early years there. Our family home was at the corner of Liberty and Yeavitz Streets, a popular residential area in those days. Jacksonville has always seemed to me the most wonderful place to live, filled with wonderful people and promising a bright future due to its geographic location on the St. Johns River and proximity to the ocean. I practiced law there for many years and witnessed its growth firsthand.

entity: Citadel, The Military College of South Carolina
explicit_text: After my education in Jacksonville schools, I attended the Citadel Military Academy in Charleston, South Carolina, from which I graduated. The Citadel provided me with a disciplined military education that shaped my early professional life. It was an important step before I returned to Jacksonville to pursue my career.

explicit_text: The Great Fire of 1901 was a devastating event for my family and me. Our home at Liberty and Yeavitz Streets was completely destroyed, along with all our papers and most of our belongings. This fire marked a significant loss in Jacksonville's history and personally affected us deeply.

explicit_text: My mother, along with Mrs. Aristides Doggett and Mrs. Alexander Mitchell, was instrumental in starting St. Luke's Hospital in Jacksonville. The first building, located at Church and Market Streets, burned down before completion, but they rebuilt it in the early 1870s on Duval Street. These women managed the hospital in its early days, providing care without trained nurses, relying on practical nurses and volunteers.

explicit_text: The St. Johns River was central to recreation and social life in my youth. Moonlight boat rides on the river were a favorite pastime, with excursions ending at places like Green Cove Springs, Middlebar, and Arlington. The river also bordered many plantations and estates, including the beautiful Villa Alexandria on its south side.

explicit_text: At the annual lawn parties held by Mrs. Alexander Mitchell for the benefit of St. Luke's Hospital, the grounds were illuminated by thousands of Japanese lanterns. This was before electric lights, so the lanterns were the sole source of lighting and created a magical atmosphere. These events were major social occasions in Jacksonville, drawing many people by chartered boats.

explicit_text: Jacksonville experienced three major yellow fever epidemics, in 1876, 1881, and 1888. Although I was not in the city during the last one, I heard much about it. People knew mosquitoes spread the disease, so those who could afford it left the city during the hot months until the first frost killed the mosquitoes, making it safe to return.

explicit_text: The Florida Yacht Club was established in 1898 and was a center for wintertime recreation. We held dances there every two weeks, which were popular social events. explicit_text: Mandarin was a collection of well-kept estates owned mostly by English families like the Bowdens, Bardins, and James family. It was beautifully developed with orange groves until a big freeze in 1864 or 1886 devastated the orange industry there. Despite this, Mandarin remained a notable part of the Jacksonville area.

explicit_text: One of the most interesting cases I prosecuted was State v. Addis Pitsler in 1896. It involved the murder of Maria Degatte, a beautiful Cuban girl and daughter of Gatte, owner of Gatte's Cigar Factory. Despite strong evidence, Pitsler was acquitted due to perjured testimony, and the trial lasted three weeks, creating intense excitement in Jacksonville.
